text.content:
Negotiation provides a structured framework for high-stakes conversations where the heroes want to convince an NPC to take a course of action. Track interest, patience, motivations, and pitfalls to make these scenes dramatic and strategic.

When to Negotiate

  • The heroes want something significant from an NPC — an artifact, military support, vital information, a powerful ally, or a plot-changing favour.
  • The NPC is conflicted — they have reason to help AND reason to refuse.
  • Simple requests (one Presence or Might test) and threats of violence are not negotiations.
  • The heroes must willingly initiate negotiation. NPCs can offer to negotiate, but heroes can always refuse.

Limits: Negotiation cannot achieve the impossible. An NPC will not act against their core nature. You cannot convince a villain to become a gardener, or a queen to hand over her crown. Negotiation works only when the request is something the NPC would seriously consider.

Threats of Violence: The potential for violence is already implicitly factored into every negotiation. If heroes explicitly threaten force, they have exited negotiation and entered a different kind of encounter — probably combat. A threatened NPC complies only while the threat is evident and will revert as soon as they can.

Stop Combat, Start Negotiation: A hero can use a manoeuvre to make a hard Presence test to halt hostilities and begin negotiation. This only works if the other side is willing and capable of negotiating — a foe with the upper hand, one who hates the heroes, or one lacking sapience is unlikely to agree.

NPC Negotiation Stats

Assign four temporary stats to the NPC before the negotiation begins:

Interest (0–5)

How eager the NPC is to make a deal. Starts between 1 and 4 based on attitude. Increases when heroes make good arguments, decreases on bad ones.

  • At 5: the NPC makes the best possible offer and the negotiation ends.
  • At 0: the NPC refuses all deals, ends the negotiation, and may become hostile.

Patience (0–5)

How long the NPC will keep talking. Starts above 0, usually decreases as arguments are made.

  • At 0: the NPC makes a final offer based on current interest and the negotiation ends.

Motivations (2+)

Topics that make arguments easier. Each motivation can be successfully appealed to only once. Appealing to a motivation = medium difficulty test.

Pitfalls (1+)

Topics that anger or alienate the NPC. Using a pitfall in an argument = automatic failure, interest −1 and patience −1. No test is made.

Starting Attitudes

Attitude Description Interest Patience
Hostile Openly opposed, barely willing to listen 1 2
Suspicious Doubts the heroes' motives but willing to listen 2 2
Neutral Indifferent, not rude, would rather be elsewhere 2 3
Open Willing to listen and help if it is not too much 3 3
Friendly Gives the heroes the benefit of the doubt 3 4
Trusting Ready to help if the heroes do not mess up 3 5

Language Bonus: If 1+ heroes can communicate in the NPC's native language (not Caelian), patience +1 (max 5). If 3+ heroes speak it, patience +2 (max 5).

Motivations & Pitfalls

Each of these twelve types can be a motivation (positive) or a pitfall (negative) for a given NPC. Assign at least 2 motivations and 1 pitfall.

Type As Motivation As Pitfall
Benevolence Wants to help others, share resources Cynical — sees charity as foolish
Discovery Seeks new knowledge, lore, exploration Fears the unknown, prefers ignorance
Freedom Desires autonomy, liberation from authority Believes authority and order are necessary
Greed Wants wealth, resources, rare items Offended by bribery, values ideals over wealth
Higher Authority Loyal to a leader, deity, or higher force Scoffs at serving anyone else
Justice Wants the righteous rewarded, the wicked punished Sees justice as a naive fantasy
Legacy Desires lasting fame and acclaim Sees pursuit of fame as vain
Peace Wants calm, stability, status quo maintained Hates boredom, craves excitement and danger
Power Wants authority, influence, expanded control Rejects the idea of ruling over others
Protection Guards people, places, or things at any cost Everyone should fend for themselves
Revelry Wants fun, parties, pleasure, and socialising Sees socialising as a waste of time
Vengeance Wants payback for past wrongs done to them Believes revenge solves nothing

Uncovering Motivations

Heroes can learn an NPC's motivations and pitfalls through:

  • Asking directly: The NPC may hint at or reveal a motivation, especially at interest 3+.
  • Making a test during negotiation: Reason, Intuition, or Presence test (see below).
  • Research beforehand: Reading diaries, talking to contacts, reconnaissance — no test during negotiation needed.

Uncovering Test (Reason, Intuition, or Presence)

Tier Result
Tier 1 (≤11) Learn nothing. NPC realises they are being read — patience −1.
Tier 2 (12–16) Learn nothing, but no penalty.
Tier 3 (17+) Learn one motivation or pitfall (hero chooses which type).

Heroes cannot attempt another uncovering test until they make an argument or the negotiation ends.

Making Arguments

One hero makes each argument (players can discuss out of character first). Arguments need both a claim and a justification. The test difficulty depends on whether the argument hits a motivation, a pitfall, or neither.

Appeal to Motivation (Medium Test)

The argument appeals to an unused motivation and avoids all pitfalls. Test: Reason, Intuition, or Presence + applicable skill.

Tier Effect
Tier 1 Patience −1
Tier 2 Interest +1, Patience −1
Tier 3 Interest +1 (patience unchanged)

At your discretion, a particularly well-roleplayed or well-reasoned argument automatically counts as Tier 3 with no test. Reward good roleplaying!

If the heroes appeal to a motivation that has already been used: interest stays the same and patience −1.

No Motivation or Pitfall (Hard Test)

The argument does not hit any motivation or pitfall.

Tier Effect
Tier 1 Interest −1, Patience −1
Tier 2 Patience −1
Tier 3 Interest +1, Patience −1 (on a natural 19–20: patience unchanged)

Repeating the same argument without a motivation or pitfall = automatic Tier 1.

Pitfall Used = Automatic Failure

If an argument invokes one of the NPC's pitfalls, it automatically fails. Interest −1, Patience −1. No test is made. The NPC warns the heroes not to go there again.

Caught in a Lie

If a hero lies in an argument that fails to increase interest, you may decide the NPC catches the lie. Additional interest −1 on top of any decrease from the failed test.

NPC Offers by Interest Level

After each argument, the NPC responds and makes an offer (or refusal) based on current interest:

Interest Offer
5 Yes, and … Everything asked for, plus extra. May waive what the heroes offered. Best possible outcome. Final offer — negotiation ends.
4 Yes. Everything asked for, no extras. Accepts the heroes' offers as-is.
3 Yes, but … Agrees to the request, but wants something extra from the heroes — a favour, payment, or additional task.
2 No, but … Cannot give what was asked, but offers something lesser in exchange for what the heroes promised.
1 No. Outright rejection, no counteroffer. May press the heroes for a better argument if patience remains.
0 No, and … Refuses everything and ends the negotiation. May become hostile — attack, spread rumours, send assassins.

Keep Going or Stop

  • After each offer, if patience > 0 and interest is 1–4, the heroes can make another argument or accept the current offer.
  • The negotiation ends when: patience = 0 (final offer), interest = 5 (best deal), interest = 0 (no deal, possible hostility), or the heroes accept or walk away.
  • Let the players drive the decision to push or accept. You can prompt with the NPC asking something like: Is there anything else?

Renown & Impression

NPCs have an Impression score (default = the NPC's level). If a hero's Renown ≥ the NPC's Impression and the NPC knows of them, the hero's fame influences the negotiation:

  • Famous to the NPC → edge on tests using Flirt, Lead, or Persuade.
  • Infamous to the NPC → edge on tests using Brag, Interrogate, or Intimidate.
Impression Example NPC
1 Brigand leader, commoner, shop owner
3 Cult leader, locally known mage, noble lord
5 Captain of the watch, high priest, viscount
7 World-renowned entertainer, marquis
9 Archmage, prince
11 Archdevil, archfey, demigod

Quick Setup Checklist

  1. Determine the NPC's attitude toward the heroes → set starting Interest and Patience.
  2. Choose 2+ motivations and 1+ pitfalls from the twelve types.
  3. Set the NPC's Impression score (usually = their level).
  4. Apply language bonus to patience if the heroes speak the NPC's native tongue.
  5. Roleplay the NPC's opening stance — what do they want? What makes them hesitant?
  6. Let the heroes make arguments. Track interest and patience after each one.
  7. After each argument, the NPC responds (positively, negatively, or impatiently) and makes an offer based on current interest.
  8. Continue until patience = 0, interest = 0 or 5, or the heroes accept or walk away.

NPC Response Cues

Unless the NPC is deceitful, make it clear whether the argument helped, hurt, or fell flat:

  • Interest increased: Positive response. The NPC engages, nods, considers the point.
  • Interest decreased: Negative response. The NPC pushes back, disagrees, looks offended.
  • Interest unchanged: Impatient response. The NPC sighs, taps their fingers, asks for something real.
